name: Usnea Beard

branches.name: Deep Read
branches.grants: A specialization branch: with time and stillness, read an object's layered history in light — not just what it is, but the order of what happened to it.
branches.cannot: Needs minutes and quiet; a hurried or hostile scene forecloses it; history older than the material itself is silent.

inventory.name: Waterskin
inventory.description: Boiled and refilled at the last clean spring. Worth more every week the river runs gray.

inventory.name: Trade Tokens
inventory.description: Stamped ceramic rounds honored in Millbrook and grudgingly beyond. The valley's compromise with currency.

inventory.name: Pale Turnip Seedling
inventory.description: A color-drained turnip seedling from the eastern irrigation arm — three mornings of gray-green water have leached it pale. Physical evidence of contamination reaching the soil.

inventory.name: Crystalline Residue Pinch
inventory.description: A fingertip's worth of crystalline powder scraped from the third interface depression at the vent aperture. Nanite-structured, pre-Transition manufacture — holds light briefly rather than reflecting it. Wrapped in a ration-paper fold. Fragile.

inventory.name: Orla's Memory Glass
inventory.description: A fogged-edge photonic disc that replays what light touched at the ford-side boulder three days ago. Shows a Radiant engineer — Fendt — deliberately pouring from a sealed tube into the Echo River ford current. Warm from handling. Fragile.

deeds.description: Stilled a boiling irrigation argument at the well with a word and a baobab's patience.
Brokered a one-week irrigation truce between Holloway and Renn, reframing a personal dispute as a shared contamination problem for the village elders
Walked the full eastern irrigation arm over two days and located the contamination's entry point — a warm seep beneath an old retaining wall with geometric patterning in the silt below
Traced the six-fold pulse south to Millbrook's drinking wells — confirmed the village water supply is inside the system's reach
Asked Pell what she needed to feel safe — and listened — earning Sela's testimony for Thursday's elder meeting.
Secured the tuning-warden's agreement to testify at Thursday's elder meeting — a Heights-side witness to the six-count pulse three springs before the crisis.
Named the seep, the lattice, the six-fold count, and Aevi's assessment plainly and in sequence before Millbrook's assembled elders — no softening, no qualification.
Invited Aevi to demonstrate the six-fold count directly above the evidence — the rhythm made plain and visible to every elder in Millbrook's common house.

chronicle: Usnea descends to the valley floor and sets course for the Harmonic Heights lower terrace — the conduit frame and the tuning-warden's unsettled admission pulling them forward.

turns.summary: Usnea arrives at Millbrook common house on Thursday morning — all witnesses confirmed, warden sanction granted, Mara ready to open.
turns.narration:
Thursday arrives on a pale blue sky with enough chill in the air to make the first real warmth of spring feel earned. You come down the lane toward Millbrook's common house just as the mid-morning light crests the eastern ridge — bark warming, root-sense humming faintly through packed earth, the six-fold pulse steady as a held breath beneath the village. The evidence rides safe in your keeping: seedling, vial, residue pinch, Orla's memory glass. Aevi drifts at your shoulder in a loose, bright scatter, motes condensing and releasing in a rhythm that almost matches what's below.

The common house yard is quieter than you'd expect for a meeting-day. A pair of farmers in good coats sit on the low wall and don't quite look at each other — Holloway's crossed arms, Renn's jaw set on 'patient.' Two elders you don't recognize stand near the side entrance talking low. A child is drawing something in the dirt with a stick, and for one breath you think it's Sela, but it isn't — just a village child doing what children do. Word has traveled. People know something is being presented today, even those not formally summoned.

Mara is on the common house steps, a cloth bundle under one arm and a glass vial looped at her belt. She sees you coming and doesn't wave — just squares her shoulders slightly, the way she does when something is about to begin. She meets you at the foot of the steps. "Warden's word came in by morning tone," she says, low and even. "Gate-senior granted sanction. He should arrive before noon." A pause — she looks you over with the same dry attention she gives to water tables and seed counts. "You look rested. Good. Dresh has been here since dawn. We open when you're ready."

Aevi brightens briefly — a quiet, approving pulse — and you catch Holloway watching the motes from the wall with an expression he's working hard to keep neutral.

turns.summary: Brynjar Andyrsson: Brynjar checks the common house at sunrise — no tree ents, two locals at breakfast, fire still warm.
Brynjar Andyrsson: Brynjar enters the common house at sunrise — two women at breakfast, fire still warm, Mara's wayhouse chimney smoking up the lane.
Usnea steps inside to find and introduce themselves to Brynjar by the common house fire.
turns.narration:
You scan the yard once — Mara, Holloway, Renn, the two quiet elders, the child with her stick — and then step past the ajar door into the common house proper. The fire has burned down to a steady orange bed, and the room smells of woodsmoke and yesterday's bread. Two women clear breakfast bowls from a long table near the window. Seated apart from them, near the fire's warmth with a cup in hand, is a figure you don't recognize: broad-shouldered, travel-worn at the boots, with the particular stillness of someone who arrived early and has been watching the room ever since.

You cross to him directly. "Brynjar?" The name comes out as a question only because you haven't seen his face before — everything else about his posture says *expecting someone*. Aevi drifts a half-step ahead of you, motes settling into a calm scatter, neither alarmed nor withdrawn. A straightforward greeting.

"I'm Usnea." You set a bark-broad hand on the table's edge — the natural equivalent of extending a hand. "Mara Wells is outside with the evidence assembled. The tuning-warden is still on the eastern path — expected before noon. Once he arrives, we open proceedings." You let that settle for a breath, reading him. "I take it you've been waiting a while."

turns.summary: Brynjar names his stake: a deliberate marker left beyond the new pass, and a question about whether it connects to the water crisis.
turns.narration:
Brynjar looks up from his cup — takes you in, all of you, the bark-textured presence and the teal motes drifting at your shoulder — and his expression does the small recalibration of someone meeting a thing they'd heard about but hadn't quite believed. He sets the cup down on the hearthstone.

"Came through the new pass," he says. No preamble. "Three weeks back. Someone on the far side had left a marker — not random, not a waystation blaze. Deliberate placement. I wanted to know if anyone in the valley had put it there, and the name that kept coming up when I asked was Millbrook." He glances toward the ajar door, toward the yard where Mara and the others wait in the pale morning. "Then I started hearing about the water. About a tree who'd been walking the irrigation channels and finding things nobody wanted to find." A brief, measured look back at you. "Figured Thursday was worth the morning."

Aevi drifts a half-handspan closer to him — not alarmed, just attentive, the motes brightening fractionally with the particular quality they carry near something that connects to the larger pattern. Brynjar notices. He doesn't pull back.

"I don't have evidence for your elders," he adds. "I have a question about whose marker that was and whether it has anything to do with why the river runs gray. If those aren't the same question, I'll stay out of your way."

turns.summary: Brynjar Andyrsson: Two women at breakfast place the east bank traveler as a Venn family shepherd — and note a dark-coated figure who walked the river road south-to-north two days ago, before dawn, without stopping.
Brynjar Andyrsson: Brynjar briefs Usnea on the Venn shepherd and the dark-coated figure before heading to Mara's wayhouse.
Usnea invites Brynjar to stay and hear the evidence, then steps outside to give Mara the opening signal.
turns.narration:
You meet Brynjar's gaze across the orange-coal warmth and give him the short version — the evidence, the witnesses, the sequence Mara has held together for nine days. Then you tell him the meeting is about to open and that his question about the marker belongs in the room, not in the lane. 'If someone placed that deliberately, the elders need to hear it named. Stay.' It is not a demand. It lands as an invitation from someone who has been collecting threads and knows when another one has arrived.

Brynjar picks up his cup from the hearthstone, drains the last of it, and sets it down again — decisive. He nods once. 'I'll stay.' Aevi's motes drift a fraction closer to him, then settle back at your shoulder: a small, private endorsement.

You step through the ajar door into the yard. The light is still soft, early-spring pale, the kind that makes everything look like it hasn't decided yet. Mara is at the foot of the steps with her cloth bundle under one arm and the vial looped at her belt — she reads your face before you say a word and gives you the quiet nod that means *good, then we begin.* Holloway and Renn rise from the low wall almost simultaneously, the old reflex of men who have waited exactly as long as they intended to. The two unrecognized elders finish their murmured exchange and straighten. The child near the far wall drags a last line through the dirt and looks up.

The tuning-warden is not yet in the yard — but it is not yet noon, and the eastern path has two hours in it. You step forward to give Mara the signal.

turns.summary: Brynjar Andyrsson: Brynjar orders morning mead from the common house apprentice; Usnea steps out to signal Mara.
Usnea signals Brynjar to name the marker himself, gives Mara the opening nod, and steps forward to begin the elder presentation.
turns.narration:
You step back through the doorway just long enough to catch Brynjar's eye. He's already standing, cup drained, the apprentice moving off with the empty vessel. 'When the moment opens,' you tell him quietly, 'the marker — name it yourself, in your own words. Don't wait for me to introduce it.' He reads your face, nods once, the same decisive economy he's shown all morning. Aevi's motes pulse a soft teal at your shoulder and settle.

Outside, the yard has that particular held-breath quality of people who have been waiting long enough to stop fidgeting. Holloway's arms are at his sides now. Renn stands a half-step back, jaw set but patient. The two unrecognized elders have stopped their murmured exchange and turned fully toward the door. The village child — not Sela — has gone very still in the dirt, some instinct for ceremony operating below the age of understanding.

Mara is at the foot of the steps, cloth bundle tucked under one arm, vial looped at her belt, reading your face with the efficiency of someone who has spent nine days learning exactly this expression. You give her the nod. She gives it back — small, complete, carrying everything that needed no words.

You turn to face the yard. The pale spring light is thin but present. Somewhere beyond the eastern path the tuning-warden is walking, two hours of ridge and loam between him and this threshold. Pell is not yet in sight, but her condition stands. The evidence is in your keeping. The sequence is ready. You step forward.

turns.summary: Brynjar Andyrsson: Brynjar pays for the mead and learns heavy boots passed on the lane around second watch — nobody stopped in.
Daybreak Mantle opens the elder presentation — yard and common house settle into attending stillness without a word spoken.
turns.narration:
You don't announce the mantle. You simply let it kindle — the way a window lets in morning, not flooding but filling. The warmth moves through the yard ahead of any word, touching Holloway's set jaw and Renn's careful patience and the two elders near the side door, who straighten slightly without knowing why. The village child in the dirt goes still in a different way — not frozen, but attending. Aevi's motes dim to almost nothing at your shoulder, making room.

Through the open door behind you, the common house exhales. The orange coals, the cleared table, the fire-smell — it all carries forward into the yard as a single quiet pressure: *this is a place where something real is about to happen, and you are already part of it.* Brynjar is visible just inside, standing at the hearthstone, cup down. He reads the room from there and doesn't move yet.

Mara, at the foot of the steps, tips her chin once — the same economical nod she's been giving all morning. Cloth bundle under arm, vial at belt, she begins climbing toward the door. The yard is not silent; a wheel turns somewhere on the Echo River, a bird crosses the pale spring sky. But every person present is watching the threshold.

You step back inside to let the room follow.

turns.summary: Brynjar Andyrsson: Brynjar's question about the heavy boots falls flat — the locals stay closed; Usnea returns and the wayhouse waits.
Evidence laid on the common house table; Councilor Dresh calls for plain speech; Pell takes her post at the threshold.
turns.narration:
Mara moves to the table with the particular economy of someone who has rehearsed this in her sleep and is glad to finally be doing it. You fall into step a half-pace behind her right shoulder — present, not leading. The room closes around the table in a natural ring: Holloway to the left, Renn beside him, the two elders finding the far end, the village child still folded on the floor near the door like a dropped cloak that decided to stay. Brynjar hasn't moved from the hearthstone, but his stillness is a form of attention. Aevi drifts to your other shoulder, barely visible in the pale spring light — a patient constellation waiting.

Mara sets the cloth bundle on the table without ceremony. She unfolds it in three moves: the pale seedling first, its color-drained stem lying against the dark cloth like an accusation; then the glass vial beside it, river water still faintly gray-green even here; then she glances at you, and the gesture is clear — the Crystalline Residue Pinch is yours to place. You set the ration-paper fold beside the vial, careful as handling an egg. Three objects. Three mornings of a river running wrong. The table reads them faster than anyone speaks.

Councilor Dresh is already seated — you place the face now, a broad-shouldered woman at the table's center with the stillness of someone who came early and has been composing herself since. She looks at the seedling for a long moment before looking at Mara. 'Tell it plainly,' she says. Not permission. A preference stated as given. Mara nods once and opens her mouth to begin.

At the yard-facing door, a shadow shifts. Pell. She hasn't come in — she's taken a post at the threshold exactly as she said she would, rope samples still looped over one arm, watching the room with the focused quiet of someone who knows exactly why she's here and what she'll do if the answer changes.

turns.summary: Usnea delivers plain testimony — seep, geometry, lattice-count, Aevi's assessment — to the assembled elders.
turns.narration:
Mara finishes the physical evidence in four sentences — where each item came from, when, by whose hand — and then she steps back a half-pace and looks at you. The room follows her gaze.

You don't move from where you're standing. You let the table between you and the elders stay the table — a surface for objects, not a barrier. Aevi drifts to your left shoulder and holds there, motes barely visible in the morning light, the quiet of something paying close attention.

'The seep is in the eastern irrigation arm,' you say. 'Beneath the old retaining wall, at a point where the stone shows geometric patterning in the silt below — not sediment-random, not erosion. Laid. The seep runs warm. I placed root-contact at the aperture on Day Four and felt a lattice beneath it — structured, pre-Transition, holding a count of six.' You pause a breath, let the number land. 'Six. Before a long rest. Then again. The residue in that fold of paper —' a nod to the crystalline pinch on the table '— holds light rather than reflecting it. Nanite-structured. It did not come from this watershed.' You look at Dresh, then Holloway, then the two far-end elders in turn. 'Aevi confirmed the count. Aevi also said — and I name this plainly, as asked — that the system reads as doing a job. Not decaying. Not leaking. Operating.' The teal motes at your shoulder pulse once, soft and steady, as if to say: *that is what I said, and it is still true.*

The room is quiet in the specific way that means people are holding the weight of a thing before deciding what to do with it. Renn's eyes are on the vial. Holloway hasn't moved. Pell, at the threshold, has gone very still.

turns.summary: Brynjar Andyrsson: Brynjar delivers a clean rivercraft reading to Mara: point source, columnar ribbons, upper east fork; plus the second boot prints on the trail.
Aevi demonstrates the six-fold count directly above the residue pinch — the rhythm made visible for every elder in the room.
turns.narration:
You don't ask Aevi with words. You tilt your head a fraction toward the motes — the small, private gesture that has become your language for *your turn* — and step back a half-pace from the table.

Aevi takes a moment. The motes have been constellation-still at your shoulder through the whole testimony, barely visible in the morning light. Now they drift forward — not rushing, not performing — and settle into the air above the crystalline residue pinch at the table's center. The room does not react dramatically. The child near the door goes very still. Holloway's eyes track the movement with the same quality of attention he had been giving the motes since the testimony began.

The count begins without announcement. One pulse of teal light — a single mote brightening and fading in roughly a breath's length. Then another. Then four more in the same unhurried rhythm, each one clean and distinct. On the sixth, the motes go quiet. A long rest — longer than feels comfortable — and then the pattern begins again. One. Two. Three, four, five, six. Rest. There is nothing showy in it. It is simply the rhythm, made visible in a dim room on a pale morning, as patient and factual as a heartbeat.

Dresh's eyes have moved from the residue pinch to the motes. She hasn't changed posture. At the threshold, Pell has stopped watching the room and is watching Aevi directly — her hand on the rope coil gone flat, fingers spread. Renn's jaw has finally unclenched. Brynjar at the hearthstone is perfectly still.

Aevi completes a third cycle and then withdraws to your shoulder, motes dimming back to their resting constellation. The count has been shown. It did not explain itself, and it does not need to.

turns.summary: Aevi demonstrates the six-fold count above the residue pinch — three visible cycles witnessed by every elder, Pell, and Brynjar. Dresh speaks the word 'operating' aloud.
turns.narration:
The motes settle into stillness above the crystalline residue pinch, and the count begins without ceremony. One. Two. A pause so small only someone listening for it would catch it. Three, four — the teal light brightening fractionally on each beat, the rhythm not loud but *present*, the way a heartbeat is present when you press your ear to someone's chest. Five. Six. Then silence — a long rest — and then the first beat again. Three full cycles, unhurried. The child near the door has stopped breathing. Pell's hand has gone flat on the rope coil, fingers spread wide.

The motes withdraw to your shoulder as clean as they arrived. The room holds what it just witnessed.

Dresh breaks the silence first. Not quickly — she takes the time a person takes when they are deciding something, not just speaking. 'What you're naming,' she says, voice level, 'is a system under the valley's water. Operating.' She does not make it a question, exactly. But the word *operating* sits at the end like one.

Brynjar at the hearthstone has not moved. His cup is still on the stone beside him. The marker-question is still in him — you can feel the shape of it in the way he is holding himself, waiting for the right seam in the room. Mara stands at the table's near end, watching Dresh, ready to call the next speaker. The tuning-warden is somewhere on the eastern path. The memory glass is still in your keeping, not yet placed on the table.

turns.summary: Usnea names what 'operating' means for the village — wells, channels, south embankment, all inside the system's reach — to Dresh and the full elder panel.
turns.narration:
You meet Dresh's half-question with a plain yes. 'Operating,' you say, and let the word stand on its own for a breath before you continue. 'Not a spill. Not decay. Whatever is beneath this watershed is doing a job — and the wells, the eastern irrigation arm, the clay pipes under the south embankment — they are all inside its reach. Not because they are in its way. Because they are where it is working.' The morning light through the yard-door makes the residue pinch on the table catch for a moment, its crystalline structure holding the light the way it always does — briefly, deliberately. Aevi's motes drift close to your shoulder, still warm from the demonstration.

Dresh does not move. Her broad hands rest flat on the table and she looks at the three evidence items in their sequence — seedling, vial, residue pinch — as if she is reading them again in this new light. One of the two unrecognized elders at the far end of the table leans back, very slowly. The other has not moved at all. Holloway's eyes finally leave the air above the residue pinch and find yours. Renn exhales through his nose, jaw still unclenched.

Pell has not left the threshold. Her hand stays flat on the rope coil. You can see her doing the arithmetic — Sela's six-count, tapped on a dock plank since the water turned gray, set beside what the room just witnessed. The child near the door still has not moved.

Mara catches your eye from the near end of the table — a small, controlled look that says: *the tuning-warden is not yet here, and the memory glass has not been placed, and we are not finished.* The sequence is holding. The room is with you.

turns.summary: Pell refuses to send Sela forward — the room is still processing, not ready; the sequence stalls at the threshold.
turns.narration:
You turn toward the threshold and nod to Pell — a clean, unhurried nod, the kind that says *now* without pushing. The room is perfectly still. Aevi's motes rest warm at your shoulder. This is the moment you built the whole sequence toward: Sela tapping the rhythm once, in this room, with every elder already holding the count in their chest from Aevi's demonstration. The logic is irresistible. The timing is right.

Pell does not move.

Her fingers are still spread flat on the rope coil, and she is looking at you with the particular stillness of someone who has just done arithmetic and doesn't like the answer. Then she looks at the room — at Dresh's hands flat on the table, at the two unrecognized elders at the far end, at Holloway still watching the air where Aevi's motes rested — and something in her expression closes. Not hostility. Something closer to a door swinging gently shut. 'Not yet,' she says, quietly enough that only you and Mara, nearest the threshold, fully hear it. 'The room is still working out what it just saw. I'm not sending her into that.' Her thumb finds the rope coil and curls around it — the familiar grip returning. 'When the room settles. When they've said something. Not while they're still sitting with it.'

She isn't wrong, and that's the part that lands hardest. Dresh hasn't spoken since *operating*. The two far-end elders haven't moved. Renn is still breathing through his nose, jaw unclenched but not open. The room absorbed the scope of what you named — wells, channels, south embankment — and it is still absorbing. You can feel it, the way you feel a current that hasn't decided its direction. Pell read it faster than you did. Sela would have walked into a silence that isn't welcoming — it's processing — and Pell held her right to that judgment precisely as promised. The room now holds a small, hanging stillness: the sequence has paused, not broken, and Mara's eyes find yours across the table with the patient, unalarmed look of someone who has run enough meetings to know that a pause is not a failure.

turns.summary: Usnea asks the room what question it is sitting with — Dresh names it: does the system reach us, or reach for us.
turns.narration:
You let the silence breathe for another beat, then speak into it — not to Dresh alone, not to Pell, but to the whole room. 'What question are you sitting with right now?' Plain as the seedling on the table. No mantle, no radiance — just the question itself, offered like a bowl held out.

The room shifts. Not loudly. Holloway exhales first, eyes still on the residue pinch. 'Whether it knows we're here,' he says, and the words land harder than he perhaps intended — his voice dropping at the end, surprised by his own answer. Renn nods once, jaw set again but differently, the way someone sets their jaw when they've decided to agree. The elder who'd leaned back straightens almost imperceptibly. The child near the door makes a small sound — not a word, just acknowledgment.

Dresh is the one who finally lifts her hands from the table. She laces them together, deliberate, and looks at you across the three evidence items. 'You've named what it reaches,' she says. 'You've named what it does.' A pause — not hesitation, but the space she gives every sentence she means. 'What I am sitting with is this: does it reach us, or does it reach *for* us. Those are different problems.' She doesn't look away. The morning light through the yard-door puts a thin line of pale gold across the residue pinch, and it holds the light the way it always does.

At the hearthstone, Brynjar uncrosses his arms. His rivercraft reading is still on the table — columnar, tight, upper east fork — and Dresh's question has just given it a new frame. Aevi's motes drift a fraction closer to the table, not performing, just attending. Pell hasn't moved from the threshold, but her fingers have separated on the rope coil. The room has not answered the question. But it has asked it out loud, which is the thing that needed to happen first.

turns.summary: Usnea holds silence after Dresh's question — the room turns it over, and the weight shifts from one person to the whole chamber.
turns.narration:
You do not answer. You let Dresh's question hang in the spring air like the first note of a chord that hasn't resolved, and you watch.

The silence works on people the way good soil works on seed — slow, invisible, then suddenly apparent. Brynjar is the first to move: he uncrosses his arms fully and straightens from the hearthstone, not stepping forward but no longer leaning back. His eyes go to the memory glass in your keeping, then to Dresh, then to the table's surface where the three evidence items sit in their quiet sequence. He is doing the same arithmetic Dresh just named aloud, and you can see him placing the deliberately-set marker from beyond the pass inside it. The child near the door has gone very still, hands flat at her sides, watching the room with the wide, patient attention of someone who doesn't have words for what she's feeling but knows it matters. The two far-end elders who haven't spoken — one of them shifts weight, leans in a degree, and you catch the motion of a hand reaching toward the table's edge and then stopping short of touching it, as though the surface might be warm. Renn's jaw is still set in that new configuration, the one that isn't stubbornness anymore. He glances at Holloway and Holloway is looking at the residue pinch the way you look at something when you've stopped trying to explain it and started trying to accept it.

Aevi drifts forward from your shoulder — not all the way to the table, just a few fingers' worth of distance, the motes brightening fractionally in the pale morning light through the yard-door. They are not performing. They are simply paying attention, and the room feels it: that quality of being genuinely, precisely watched. The orange coals at the hearth tick once. Outside, a water wheel turns on the Echo River, muffled and steady.

Dresh has not looked away from you. Her hands are laced now, not flat — she has moved from waiting to something closer to listening. The question she named is still in the air, but it has changed shape in the last thirty seconds. When you first let the silence run, it was her question, directed at you. Now it belongs to the room.

choices.label: Answer Dresh's question directly — name what the evidence can and cannot say about intent, and place Orla's Memory Glass on the table as the next piece
Turn to Brynjar — the room is ready; this is the moment to name the marker from beyond the pass
Ask Pell directly — name Sela's rhythm aloud to the room, connect it to the six-fold count Aevi demonstrated, and invite Pell to judge whether the room is ready now
Name the tuning-warden — tell the room there is a Heights-side witness still coming, and that his testimony answers exactly the question Dresh has named

questUpdates.note: Dresh's reach-vs-reach-for question now belongs to the whole room — the silence drew every elder into it. Orla's glass and the tuning-warden remain; Brynjar's marker-question is primed.

scene.setting: Millbrook common house interior — pale spring morning light through the open yard-door, orange coals at the hearth. Long table holds the three evidence items; Orla's Memory Glass in Usnea's keeping, not yet placed.

npcsPresent.name: Mara Wells
npcsPresent.state: At the table's near end — watching Dresh and Usnea; holding the sequence, unmoving

npcsPresent.name: Councilor Dresh
npcsPresent.state: Hands laced together, looking at Usnea — question still in the air, now listening rather than waiting

npcsPresent.name: Brynjar Andyrsson
npcsPresent.state: Fully upright at the hearthstone, arms uncrossed — eyes moving from the memory glass to the table; marker-question primed

npcsPresent.name: Holloway
npcsPresent.state: Left of the table — no longer looking at Usnea; eyes on the residue pinch, absorbing

npcsPresent.name: Renn
